rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank. Alaska must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt). Calibrated to methodology anchors and to the six states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Alaska at 47.5 sits between Ohio and Michigan.
attribution_note: Scores Alaska state conduct only. SNAP is federally funded but state-administered, so the processing backlog is state discretion. Newtok relocation was mostly federally funded (IIJA $25M, FEMA $6.7M) with the state providing technical assistance, so state credit is limited. HB 123 tribal recognition was a discretionary state statute and is fully credited.
key_evidence: Only US state whose statewide health improvement plan (Healthy Alaskans 2030) is co-led by the state and the Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium; HB 123 (2022) recognized 229 tribes in statute; Tribal Child Welfare Compact transfers child welfare delivery to tribal organizations.

Federal court ordered Alaska on 2024-12-31 to process SNAP applications within legal timeframes; backlog still persisted December 2025; state then won summary judgment on 2026-05-21 by arguing SNAP creates no privately enforceable rights.

Department of Corrections dissolved its internal in-custody death investigation unit in 2018; 84 in-custody deaths since 2020 and a record-tying 18 in 2025, with no independent oversight body restored.

June 2026 vetoes cut $11.25M in Medicaid provider payments, $6.4M for child care workforce, plus early intervention, infant learning and Head Start; VPSO recruitment funding cut to $15,775 of $55,775 requested while citing officer vacancies.
2025 legislative audit found $20.7M and 110 new positions did not effectively increase services for Alaskan children; 70% of experienced caseworkers exceeded the 2018 statutory caseload cap.
